## Provide better aggregate protection for groups of pods with bad probes

### What would you like to be added?

Prevent liveness probes from driving all pods in a cluster down (by treating a container restart as a gated, voluntary disruption)

### Why is this needed?

Overly sensitive liveness probes have the potential to cause outage.  Categorizing restarts as voluntary disruptions that must be budget checked provides natural gating to protect Service availability.
assistant:
> might create more simultaneous restarts than I'm comfortable with

I ACK this problem, but offer questions in response.

What if those containers are legitimately crash?  You're going to restart anyway.  We used to call that the Query Of Death.  A well-meaning client accidentally triggers a panic/segfault, so the query fails.  The client or the LB sends it again, to a new backend and repeat.

If the LivenessProbe fails, that's *direct* evidence that the pod is in trouble.  It's not responding to even the most rudimentary queries.  For all intents - it's dead.  Now, hypothetically it could recover in the next millisecond, but we don't know that.  You, the definer of the probe, chose that timeout and that failure count.  Why should we need to check beyond that?

Practically speaking, you're sort of describing a token-bucket for pod restarts, right?  Kubelet checks if it can get permission to restart a pod, where the answer is either "yes" or "ask again soon".  We don't have an easy way to do that.

> if your goal is to stop too many containers for a given Deployment restart at once

If you failed a probe, I would argue we're not "stopping" you - you are already stopped.
[truncated]

---

> You are pre-supposing that pod authors are effective at determining that "the pods is in trouble"

I have to assume the author of the pod knows more about it than I do, right?

> But if all the pods of the same type get it wrong in the same way at the same time? Then, well, you're not having a very good day.

Counter-argument: I'd rather know about it so I can maybe catch it in testing) than have something try to paper over it.

> The original ask was to budget-check restarts. I take your point that that's difficult to achieve.

It's not that it's super technically hard, but a) we don't have a mechanism (we have no self-refilling token-bucket API); b) I don't know if we have precedent for something like this (leases, maybe?); c) we end up with another config that has to be correct, in another API.  If users can't get liveness probes right, what makes us think they would get this right?

>  I'd like to be able to set 'repairMode: attemptEviction' on a livenessProbe. In that mode, if the probe crosses the repair threshold, we call the pod eviction api.

I'm not against an escalating response to repeatedly-failing probes, but ultimately that is a hyper-local decision, 
[truncated]

---

> we want to give app owners the ability to choose to protect themselves ... from themselves.

How do *we* (who did not craft the pod and know nothing about what it is running) decide when a bunch of pods all legitimately died at roughly the same time (maybe they have a memory leak which correlates to time, or some time-base bug which causes an livelock) vs. a dodgy probe that we should ignore?  We could just make it all worse.  From our POV, the signal is pretty identical.  Treating a mass-death as a susipicious even might end up injecting jitter into pod startup, so time-based issues would eventually fall out of phase lock?

If we restart the first 500, but then hit a "> 50% is fishy" trigger, the other 500 will not restart right away.  Assume we add some increasing jitter to them, like 10s of seconds.  Now we have one cohort that is phase-locked and one that is sort of spread out over a longer time window.  That will show up as a huge spike, followed by a constant low-rate of failure with occasional spikes of up to 50% of capacity.

Is that really better than just restarti
[truncated]

## Allow pods to enter host's cgroup namespace

#### What would you like to be added:

Kubernetes currently exposes `hostPID` and `hostNetwork` configurations for pods to enter host's PID and network namespaces respectively. Allow pods to enter host's cgroup namespace as well. 

#### Why is this needed:

CNIs like Cilium attach BPF programs to cgroups to enable [socket-based load-balancing](https://docs.cilium.io/en/v1.10/gettingstarted/kubeproxy-free/) using eBPF. BPF cgroup programs can also be used to collect [statistics](https://kubernetes.io/blog/2017/12/using-ebpf-in-kubernetes/#pod-and-container-level-network-statistics). In order to do that, CNI agents mount cgroup2 filesystem on the underlying kubernetes node. With cgroup v2 adoption, container runtimes have switched the default cgroup namespace mode to [private](https://docs.docker.com/config/containers/runmetrics/#running-docker-on-cgroup-v2). In this mode, every pod has its own cgroup namespace. As a result, CNI agents end up attaching eBPF programs at their virtualized cgroup root, and the attached eBPF programs aren't effective for other pods. 
In Cilium, we've worked around this problem by mounting cgroup2 fs on the underlying host in an init container using `nsenter` command, with cgroup and mount namespaces as enterable. However, this requires host's `/proc` to be mounted in an init container, and thus, may have security implications. Alternatively, we also allow users to specify mount point on the host where cgroup2 fs is already mounted, and we vo
[truncated]

CNI daemonsets are also already quite privileged typically including e.g. writing executables to the host (to be invoked by the container runtime) and controlling in-cluster networking ...

## Race condition in Kubelet service

@kubernetes/sig-api-machinery-bugs

 **What happened**:

Race condition in Kubelet causes Kubernetes cluster to become unstable due to high CPU load when two nodes share the same hostname or `--hostname-override` value.

**What you expected to happen**:

Since node hostname or `--hostname-override` value is used to compute the etcd key name (`/registry/minions/[NODE-HOSTNAME]`), the system should either:

1. prevent nodes with a duplicate hostname or `--hostname-override` value to join the cluster,
2. add a prefix/suffix to the etcd key name (e.g. `/registry/minions/[NODE-HOSTNAME]-[SUFFIX]`)

**How to reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)**:

1. Spawn 3 new servers
2. Setup Kubernetes on all 3 servers, according to the following table:

   | Server ID |  Hostname  | Cluster Role |
   | :-------: | :--------: | :----------: |
   |     0     | k8s-master |     Master   |
   |     1     | k8s-node-1 |     Worker   |
   |     2     | k8s-node-2 |     Worker   |
3. Start all 3 servers and initialize the cluster
4. Access the server w/ ID 1 and change its hostname to `k8s-master`
5. Run the `kubeadm join` with appropriate flags and tokens.

   You should see on `stdout` the following messages
   [long code block omitted]
  Client Version: version.Info{Major:"1", Minor:"14", GitVersion:"v1.14.3", GitCommit:"5e53fd6bc17c0dec8434817e69b04a25d8ae0ff0", GitTreeState:"clean", BuildDate:"2019-06-06T01:44:30Z", GoVersion:"go1.12.5", Compiler:"g
[truncated]

this seems like the correct course. the "newer" kubelet should exit with an error.

Server Version: version.Info{Major:"1", Minor:"14", GitVersion:"v1.14.3", GitCommit:"5e53fd6bc17c0dec8434817e69b04a25d8ae0ff0", GitTreeState:"clean", BuildDate:"2019-06-06T01:36:19Z", GoVersion:"go1.12.5", Compiler:"gc", Platform:"linux/amd64"}

what is the state in 1.15 and master?

---

The name of the API object is unique, is limited to a fixed character set, and is reasonable to use as the database key. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names/ for details about the meaning of API object names.

> let's get back to the given exploitation scenario where an attacker, who was able to break out of a container and execute code on the host OS with superuser privileges, will be able to take down the cluster, using the controlled node to create a hostname collision (note that the node is already a member of the cluster).

A node that is already joined to the cluster does not gain the ability to modify other Node objects (and therefore trigger this collision) simply by changing its hostname. Recommended procedure for joining nodes to the cluster does not leave superuser credentials accessible to the node. A properly secured node only has the ability to modify the status of its own Node API object (limited by the credentials held by the kubelet, which are pinned to its original Node name). It is the responsibility of the cluster administrator to avoid collisions when joining nodes to their cluster.

I would be interested in the nod
[truncated]

---

an existing Node API object does not mean there is a competing kubelet. it **usually** means the kubelet restarted on a Node whose configuration was modified and it is expected to update the API object to match current state.

> Is this "responsibility" a manual thing?

the deployment procedure should allocate unique hostnames. if that is not true, a unique name should be computed and given to the deployment procedure to construct the kubelet invocation.

> Does this answer your question?
> If not, can you please provide me guidance to collect required data to answer your question?

Not really. It would be helpful to capture the sequential updates to the node object to see how quickly they are occurring and what changes are being made.

Capturing the following would help:
1. api server audit log (filtered to requests from the relevant nodes)
2. kubelet logs for the relevant nodes
3. updates to the node API object (`kubectl get nodes/<name> -w -o json > nodes.json`)

## Topology Aware Hint does not consider node taints

### What happened?

Consider a K8S cluster which is spread across several zones. In each zone there can be many `node-groups` which is the unit for `Cluster-Autoscaler`. Typically you would create different node groups either with different node labels or taints which will help the Cluster Autoscaler to scale a specific node group (optionally in a specific zone) in case there are unscheduled pods or over-utilised nodes.
[image]

In the above setup for nodes in each node group across zones taints are added so that workloads which have tolerations to the taint can be scheduled across zones.

Also consider the following `Allocatable CPU` for each node across node groups/zones:
[image]

Now consider a service whose pods are only deployed on nodes where the `taint = T1`. This will target node groups: `NodeGroup-A1T1`, `NodeGroup-B1T1` and `NodeGroup-C1T1`. If you look at the cpu ratio per zone then as per the current implementation of `endpointslice controller` the hints will be removed. 
**Why is this the case?**
It is because: cpu-ratios of Zone-A is almost twice as large as cpu-ratio of Zone-B.
In [topologycache.go](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/23144130a815117194afbf3f703024ed76d5dac1/pkg/controller/endpointslice/topologycache/topologycache.go#L256-L258) method `getAllocations` will return `nil` which will cause any hints to be removed.

Cluster-Autoscaler can scale each node group at anytime resulting in change of cpu-ratios in a zone. This furthe
[truncated]
assistant:
>I'm grumpy about adding APIs for this, so that's my least-favorite choice, but I am open to it if we really REALLY can't do better.

So the original "Auto" mode for topology assumes that the user has not or can not ensure that pods are distributed evenly across zones. I think there is a subset of users that want to be able to say "keep my traffic in the same zone, I'll make sure my workloads are evenly distributed". Fundamentally I think this requires a separate deployment + HPA per zone today. We could either make that a prerequisite of the "Auto" approach (probably a breaking change), or we could make a separate option that relies on this. Similar to the problems/limitations of the current approach, this does not work well when there are a small number of pods, say 4 pods distributed over 3 zones, but this kind of user configuration would be more predictable when it comes to autoscaling.

With the current approach, it's not obvious from an autoscaling perspective which Pods are serving traffic for which zones. So even if you had per-zone HPAs and Deployments set up, there's still a chance that one of your pods from one zone would get allocated to another zone, and autoscalin
[truncated]

---

+1 to the comments from @thockin and @aojea. I'm very interested in better heuristics here, but not sure how to improve this without creating other problems. Open to ideas from anyone here.

I'm not sure it's accurate to consider this a bug though, more of a known and documented limitation of this approach. Currently the [relevant docs](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/topology-aware-hints/#constraints) say the following:

>The EndpointSlice controller does not take into account [tolerations](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/scheduling-eviction/taint-and-toleration/) when deploying calculating the proportions of each zone. If the Pods backing a Service are limited to a subset of Nodes in the cluster, this will not be taken into account.

---

> It also currently also considers nodes which are not ready or not healthy where pods cannot be scheduled but the allocated cpus will be added.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/18b5efceda2643b7175fbf712e8978d382077f9b/pkg/controller/endpointslice/topologycache/topologycache.go#L177-L185

## Report CPU usageNanoCores for pods/containers in summary API on Windows

Related: #62047

On Windows, kubelet reports only the cumulative`usageCoreNanoSeconds` for cpu usage.
[long code block omitted]

On Linux, kubelet reports both `usageCoreNanoSeconds` and the instantaneous `usageNanoCores`.
```
    "containers": [
     {
      "name": "foo",
      "startTime": "2019-01-09T23:18:42Z",
      "cpu": {
       "time": "2019-01-10T01:08:29Z",
       "usageNanoCores": 2825438,
       "usageCoreNanoSeconds": 19549406996
      },
```

Although In the future, most components/monitoring pipelines will switch to using the cumulative metrics, a few features still depend on the instantaneous `usageNanoCores` metrics today. Examples are: `kubectl top`, HPA/VPA. Kubelet should report this metrics on Windows to support the existing features.

/cc @dashpole, who explained to me what features depend on the cpu metrics.
/cc @kubernetes/sig-windows-feature-requests @PatrickLang @feiskyer 
/cc @pjh @mtaufen
assistant:
Currently, `usageCoreNanoSeconds` is got from docker's stats API on Windows. From docker stats API [here](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/vendor/github.com/docker/docker/api/types/stats.go#L23), only `TotalUsage` is available, hence `usageNanoCores` is not set on Windows:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/8b98e802eddb9f478ff7d991a2f72f60c165388a/vendor/github.com/docker/docker/api/types/stats.go#L18-L40

@PatrickLang @michmike Do you know any way to get instantaneous `usageNanoCores` for Windows containers?

---

Documentation on metrics API and metrics server (i.e., the aggregator): https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/core-metrics-pipeline/

The metrics-server pod keeps spewing errors like this:
```
unable to fully collect metrics: [unable to fully scrape metrics from source kubelet_summary:e2e-test-yjhong-minion-windows-group-7gg8...
```
Running `kubectl top pod`:
```console
# Against a Linux pod
$ kubectl top pod ubuntu
NAME      CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)   
ubuntu    0m           0Mi    

# Against a Windows pod
$ kubectl top pod foo-server
Error from server (NotFound): podmetrics.metrics.k8s.io "default/foo-server" not found
```
I haven't verified whether pod autoscaling works in my cluster, but I assume they won't since metrics server seems to discard the metrics from the windows pods.

> @feiskyer - is this something that's clear enough in the CRI layer that you can implement it, or do we need to fix that first?

I believe the implementation is in kubelet (for the docker integration), so this should not need CRI changes.

---

As to how this could be done, in [pkg/kugelet/stats/cri_stats_provider.go:ListPodStats()](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/kubelet/stats/cri_stats_provider.go#L78) and 
 [ListPodCPUAndMemoryStats()](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/kubelet/stats/cri_stats_provider.go#L178) we could just add a `map[string]*runtimeapi.CpuUsage` in the `criStatsProvider` struct, which we assign the previous CpuUsage metric for each container.  Then, when we need to calculate the rate, we pass the previous CpuUsage along with the current one to `makeContainerStats`, and calculate a rate based on the timestamp and values.

## Memory based autoscaler

Hey!

I've been working on integrating Kubernetes and Infinispan (Java based Data Grid, distributed cache,  in-memory key/value store) and I noticed that [Kubernetes horizontal pod autoscaling feature](http://kubernetes.io/docs/user-guide/horizontal-pod-autoscaling) operates only on CPU metrics. 

I would like to propose extending the Autoscaler's functionality and adding memory based thresholds (I understand that a similar effect might be achieved by using Custom Metrics but it requires a lot more configuration). The Autoscaler could possibly be configured to use a combination of two - CPU and memory metrics (but this needs a longer thought how to avoid flipping the number of replicas, probably the easiest way is to set priorities to the metrics).

N.B. Since there are usually 3 types of applications - CPU, memory and I/O intensive, we could add 3rd metric to the Autoscaler - I/O. However I'm not sure how this fits into Persistent Volumes concept in Kubernetes.

What do you think about this idea?

Thanks
Sebastian
assistant:
Auto-scaling base on memory usually does not work correctly. Memory consumption of pods usually never shrinks, so, adding a new pod will not decrease memory consumption by old pods. This is the reason why there was no autoscaling base on memory in the initial version of HPA.

It is possible that in future scaling based on memory will be possible by explicitly specifying one of embedded hepster metrics (see #28628).

---

Update: memory autoscaling and custom metrics autoscaling are in beta as of Kubernetes 1.8.  I think we can close this issue.

---

I think it depends on application type. Data Grids for an instance usually do a rebalance after adding a new node, so you might expect that the total amount of memory used in all pods will slightly drop. Of course it needs proper configuration, in case of JVM it requires setting at least `Xms` (as described [here](https://developerblog.redhat.com/2014/07/15/dude-wheres-my-paas-memory-tuning-javas-footprint-in-openshift-part-1/) and [here](https://developerblog.redhat.com/2014/07/15/dude-wheres-my-paas-memory-tuning-javas-footprint-in-openshift-part-2)).

Could you please tell me if there are any plans of unifying HPA and using https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/28628 for CPU as well? Otherwise there would be a dichotomy (for CPU use this and for anything else use that).

## Scheduler pre-binding can cause race conditions with automated empty node removal

### What happened?

In a Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) environment, a pod was requesting a large Persistent Volume Claim (PVC). After the appropriate node was identified for the pod, the pod became stuck in the prebinding stage for several minutes while the volume provisioning process completed.  Since the node name was not assigned to the pod during this time, the Cluster Autoscaler perceived the node as unoccupied. Consequently, the Cluster Autoscaler initiated a scale-down of the node, unaware that the pending pod was scheduled to run there.

### What did you expect to happen?

I would expect that the Scheduler would communicate the intended binding of the pod to the identified node. This would enable the Cluster Autoscaler to recognize that the node is not actually empty and prevent it from being scaled down prematurely.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

The issue arose in a large GKE cluster with pods requesting substantial PVCs, making replication potentially challenging. However, the race condition within the Scheduler is evident.

Let me share my first thought about the issue. Forgive me my limited knowledge of the system, but I'm ramping up.

The race between scheduler context and autoscaling (downsizing) seems pretty obvious. When more scheduling decisions are decoupled from the actuation (like https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/126858), there's a bigger likelihood of attempting to follow a wrong decision.

I think that there are two main approaches we could take to address the issues like this:
1. Detect wrong scheduling decisions without unnecessary delay and correct them in the next cycle
2. Reduce the race condition window to the minimum between components which can interfere with each other

I suspect that with a growing cluster scales, there will be growing number of inconsistencies the scheduler may run into. Scheduler and autoscaler is probably only one of such examples, but for instance pod vertical scaling might be potential future one, so maybe we should consider exploring the first approach, which should work with all potential future issues.

## Inconsistent Job state on DeadlineExceeded

### What happened?

We have a maintenance task running in our clusters to delete obsolete jobs. The task uses a field selector to list completed jobs, `status.successful=1`, and then uses the `.status.completionTime` field to decide if the job should be deleted. This has been running fine for a long time, but suddenly the task started crashing in one of our clusters. After some initial investigation it turns out that this is caused by `.status.completionTime` being unset, even if the job appeared to have been completed successfully, based on `status.successful`/`status.succeeded`.

After removing irrelevant fields, the job may look like this:

assistant:
@erikgb As we discussed this, we decided not to reorder the rules evaluation flow.
The primary reasons are described here (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/121863#issuecomment-2447872050). In conclusion, even if we reverse the evaluation order (positive check vs negative check), we face the similar issue (why Job does not fail even if Job reached positive rules).

Additionally, this issue's root cause is relying on the `status.succeeded` to detect the Job successful. The `.status.succeeded` is the intermediate state for the JobController, so we do not guarantee that the field represents the Job completion. So, we would recommend to rely on the `.status.completionTime` as @alculquicondor suggestion in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/117303#issuecomment-1531726827.

However, I agree with the lack of documentation for this behavior. Hence, we will work on improving the documentation for a better understanding of Job rules evaluations at https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/128564.

Thank you for this meaningful reporting!

cc: @mimowo @atiratree @soltysh 

/remove-kind bug
/kind support
/close

---

> We calculate the deadline before calculating the number of succeeded pods. That's why you observe that behavior.
We just need to swap the order of those calculations.

@alculquicondor I was not able to reproduce this issue. But I think the reason here is we calculate the deadline **after** the succeeded pods, not **before** ?

calculate the number of succeeded pods:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a95a79c785ec449074afc689306d1155aa039025/pkg/controller/job/job_controller.go#L792

calculate the deadline:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a95a79c785ec449074afc689306d1155aa039025/pkg/controller/job/job_controller.go#L825

Also, since the pod posted in this issue has a succeeded phase. I think we'll count it as succeed no matter what the calculation order is. So how will  `swap the order` take effect? Can you explain it in more detail?😂

---

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/42799903af83b38777e5a0e132716770e1d9473b/pkg/controller/job/job_controller.go#L1235-L1237

We don't set `completionTime` when the job fails. And this is not a regression. It was the same in v1.22:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/bc4763cbf8b9e6adfbc07bb3194509b187b0b901/pkg/controller/job/job_controller.go#L1077

The field `completionTime` was probably added before we had the concept of conditions. Since we have the conditions, there is no point in changing the behavior of `completionTime`. I would suggest:
- Check the timestamp in the conditions instead.
- Use ttlAfterFinished, so you don't have to manually clean the jobs. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/ttlafterfinished/

/close

## Full Pod Disruption Budget support for Daemonsets

### What would you like to be added?

Pod Disruption Budgets to have full support for Daemonsets. Currently only [minAvailable with integer values](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/configure-pdb/#arbitrary-controllers-and-selectors) is supported.

### Why is this needed?

Daemonsets are used for cluster level networking functions like Ingress and Kubernetes system components like kube-proxy. Pod Disruption Budget support allows proper signaling that it is not safe to restart all pods in the Daemonset simultaneously. With the current available implementation there are situations where tools like the [Vertical Pod Autoscaler](https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/tree/master/vertical-pod-autoscaler) will evict all pods at the same time in an undesirable way.

### Background

Similar to as seen by [Nginxinc](https://github.com/nginxinc/kubernetes-ingress/blob/master/deployments/daemon-set/nginx-ingress.yaml) we currently run our Nginx Ingress as a Daemonset. This is done for two reasons:

1. To work around an issue in GKE where it is not possible for nodes to reach an ingress running within the cluster unless the ingress pod is on their node (we run a Docker registry in GKE).
2. It allows us to naturally scale our ingress workload with the amount of nodes in the cluster. Without the workaround needed from 1 we would use a deployment with Horizontal Pod Autoscaler.

As a result of this we now use the [Vertical Pod Autoscaler](https://github.com/k

HPA based on CPU makes two assumptions.

1. CPU varies with direct proportion to QPS
1. QPS is load balanced, and when auto scaling occurs, the increased capacity of a new Pod will decrease the per Pod QPS, and therefore will decrease the per Pod CPU utilization.

Stateful workloads may violate .1. For example, log structured merge trees and append only btrees may cause CPU spikes during compaction that are only loosely correlated with QPS and that do not vary with direct proportion to the current QPS.

More importantly, for distributed storage applications, when you increase the number of processes in the system by increasing the number of Pods, you may trigger replication or replica re-balancing (depending on the nature of the stateful application). This is likely to increase network and CPU utilization in the short term. Only after the re-balance or replication is complete would you be able to make use of the additional capacity offered by the new Pods. 

For synchronously and asynchronously replicated ACID systems, reducing the number or replicas based on CPU utilization might be safe, but, for BASE systems,  scaling down will also trigger a re-balance (so the systems 
[truncated]

---

@gyliu513 This isn't a bug. Autoscaling isn't implemented for StatefulSets or DaemonSets.  Auto-scaling a stateless application based on cpu utilization generally makes sense. Autoscaling a stateful workload based on that criteria can often do more harm (at least in the short term) than good. If you are asking for an implementation of horizontal Pod auto-scaling for StatefulSet you should open this as a feature request.

---

@kow3ns  I think Statefulset can't meet the requirements of applications like database、distributed storage service or even spark. Those applications is too complicate to fit in statefulset. They should be manipulated by customized controller through CRD, like what so many Operators do.
We need a controller which provides persistent identifier for pods, so we can create reference between pods and other resource persistently. It is reasonable to provide ability of horizontally auto scaling for such types of applications.

## Remove usage of deprecated klog flags from Kubernetes Org repos

Klog specific flags are deprecated as part of https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/2845

All references to deprecated flags in k8s codebase should be removed or migrated to go-runner. This issue will track the work related on it.

What needs to be done:
* Check all occurrences of deprecated flag names and verify if they are affected
  * Affected cases are those where this flag is passed to K8s binary using component-base binary (kube-apiserver, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, kube-proxy, etc)
* Propose reasonable migration path depending on flag used
  * --log-file should use go-runner 
  ...
* Apply migration in file  

List of files to be migrated based on [search](https://cs.k8s.io/?q=%22log-dir%7Clog-file%7Cone-output%7Cstderrthreshold%7Clog-file-max-size%7Cskip-log-headers%7Cadd-dir-header%7Cskip-headers%7Clog-backtrace-at%22&i=nope&files=&excludeFiles=%5C.md%24%7C.*vendor%2F&repos=kubernetes-client/.github,kubernetes-client/c,kubernetes-client/csharp,kubernetes-client/gen,kubernetes-client/go,kubernetes-client/go-base,kubernetes-client/haskell,kubernetes-client/java,kubernetes-client/javascript,kubernetes-client/perl,kubernetes-client/python,kubernetes-client/python-base,kubernetes-client/ruby,kubernetes-csi/.github,kubernetes-csi/cluster-driver-registrar,kubernetes-csi/csi-driver-host-path,kubernetes-csi/csi-driver-image-populator,kubernetes-csi/csi-driver-iscsi,kubernetes-csi/csi-driver-nfs,kubernetes-csi/csi-driver-nvmf,kubernetes-cs
[truncated]
assistant:
@pohly One problem with proposing deprecating klog flags in component-base is that there are multiple components that don't use go-runner. Kubernetes core components already have go-runner available and are using them default manifests. For components like cluster-autoscaler and kube-proxy we might need to do additional work.

---

From the description:

> Affected cases are those where this flag is passed to K8s binary using component-base binary (kube-apiserver, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, kube-proxy, etc)

This "using component-base" needs to be removed. We are deprecating klog in *all* of Kubernetes, not just in some binaries.

---

We are not yet deprecating klog, as we don't have replacement ready yet. For now we are deprecating klog specific features that are enabled via it's flags. We need to avoid wrong messaging to prevent confusion. 

We are deprecating klog specific flags in Kubernetes Org projects, but should not affect:
* projects just using klog

## Which Kubernetes components or activities use/can use monitoring data from the cluster?

Hi all,

As part of a development project I am currently diving a bit deeper into the topic of monitoring in Kubernetes. I have no issues with the setup and monitoring pipelines. I have both the metrics server and Prometheus monitoring up and running fine. However, as the topic states, I would like to know which Kubernetes components use/can use monitoring data from the cluster.

_What do I mean with "Kubernetes components or activities"?_
Obviously, one of the main use cases for monitoring data are all autoscaling mechanisms, including Horizontal Pod Autoscaler, Vertical Pod Autoscaler and Cluster Autoscaler. I am searching for further components or activities, which use live monitoring data from a Kubernetes cluster, and potentially a short explanation why they use it (if it is not obvious). Also it would be interesting to know, which of those components or activities must work with monitoring data and which can work with monitoring data, i.e. can be configured to work with monitoring data.

_What do I mean with "monitoring data"?_
Monitoring data include, but are not limited to: Node metrics, Pod metrics, Container metrics, network metrics and custom/application-specific metrics (e.g. captured and exposed by third-party tools like Prometheus).

With this issue, I hope a get some insight from different Kubernetes users or sigs. I am thankful for every answer or comment in advance!

## Vertical pod auto-sizer

We should create a vertical auto-sizer.  A vertical auto-sizer sets the compute resource limits and request for pods which do not have them set, and periodically adjust them based on demand signals.  It does not directly deal with replication controllers, services, or nodes.

Related issues:
- #2768
- #147
- #1629
- #10242 
-  #1899

Especially when the estimation is wrong and the user would observe unexpected kill of his container. So that we need to have high confidence while setting Limits and we can't guarantee it from the beginning.

I think everyone agrees that setting Request should improve the overall experience which may not be true for setting Limits. Long term we definitely want to set both, but I would set only Request in the first version (which may be different then v1.1), gather some feedback from users and then eventually add setting Limits once we will have algorithm tuned.

@vishh How about having two containers that exceed their request: which one will be killed? The one that exceeds request 'more' or random one?

---

We are planning to divide vertical pod autoscaling into three implementation steps. We are going to deliver the first of them, **Setting Initial Resources**, for version 1.1.

### Setting Initial Resources

Setting initial resources will be implemented as an admission plugin. It will try to estimate and set values for **request** memory/CPU for containers within each pod if they were not given by user. (The plugin will not set **limit** to avoid OOM killing).

We will additionally annotate containers metrics with image name. Usage for given image will be aggregated (it is not yet decided how and by whom) and initial resource plugin will set **request** base on the aggregation.

### Reactive vertical autoscaling by deployment update

We will add a new object: vertical pod autoscaler, which will work on a deployment level. User, when specifying a pod template in a deployment, will have an option to set _enable_vertical_autoscaler_ flag. If the auto flag is given, vertical pod autoscaler will monitor resource usage of pod’s containers and change they resource requirements by updating the pod’s template in the deployment object. So, the deployment will act as the actuator of the autosc

## Improved memory usage measurements

A container's memory limit is implemented by setting `/sys/fs/cgroup/memory/$CGROUP_PATH/memory.limit_in_bytes`.

In order for a vertical pod autoscaler (#10782) or a human to set a memory limit accurately, an accurate measurement of memory usage is needed.

"Needed" is not the same as "usage".  We want to use "Needed" so that, for example, we don't count pages that were read at startup but aren't needed anymore, but haven't been freed because there happened to be no memory pressure on the machine.

Currently, we use cadvisor to tell us this "needed" number, which cadvisor calls "MemoryStats.WorkingSet".  

Cadvisor gets this from `memory.stat`, and in particular it subtracts `total_inactive_anon` and `total_inactive_file` from the total usage, [here](https://github.com/google/cadvisor/blob/4a9a57cdd061e241a974602009f26729f719a862/container/libcontainer/helpers.go#L191).

This is good, but it has some problems.

## Local Storage Request/Limit Validation Discussion

## Feature: local storage request/limit
From v1.7, we add the feature to allow users set local storage limit for pod’s emptyDir and container’s writable layer (overlay) to better provide storage capacity isolation.

For all type of resources in Kubernetes, user can set value for request and/or limit. The relationship between request and limit is defined as follows 

- A BestEffort quality of service is provided when a request and limit are not specified.
- A Burstable quality of service is provided when a request is specified that is less than an optionally specified limit.
- A Guaranteed quality of service is provided when a limit is specified that is equal to an optionally specified request.

The quality of service has an impact on what happens if the resource is compressible or not. For incompressible resource such as memory,  a BestEffort container will be able to consume as much resource as is available on the node. In addition, a BestEffort container has the greatest chance of being killed if there is an out of resource event on the node. A Burstable container will be scheduled on the node to get the amount of resource requested, but it may consume more. If there is an out of resource event on the node, containers of this type will be killed after BestEffort containers when attempting to reclaim resource. A Guaranteed container will get the amount of resource requested, but no more. In the event of a system out of resource event, it will only be killed if there
[truncated]

## Online resource oversubscription and external QoS control

Some jobs in the cluster are allocated resources (i.e., provisioned) to handle maximum loads and unexpected load spikes. As a result, a cluster might remain under-utilized. The cluster utilization can be improved if these temporarily unused resources from over-provisioned jobs can be used for running best-effort jobs. On the other hand, allowing many best-effort jobs jobs to run without any QoS control can easily lead to service-level objective (SLO) violation of the original job that was running on the node. 

**Proposal**
An external QoS controller can continuously monitor the resource usage and resource allocated to each pod in the Kubernetes cluster and calculate the usage slack (i.e., the difference between the resources allocated and the resources used). Based on the slack, the controller can perform actions to allow online resource oversubscription. 

For a more in-depth discussion of the requirements, mechanisms and proposed changes please refer to (and also comment/edit) this shared document [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1uulT2AjqXjc_pLtDu0Kw9WyvvXm-WAZZaSiUziKsr68/edit?usp=sharing).

After discussing and solidifying the ideas outlined in the document, we can add them to the docs/proposals and/or docs/design directories. Advice from maintainers on what belongs where is welcome.

**Upstream Work Items**
[#28316 External QoS Controller and Implement resizing a Pod/Container resource envelope via update in the API server.](https://github.com/kubernetes/kuber
[truncated]

My hope is that some day we can unify some of the implementations in Kubernetes where we could benefit from greater sharing, but I think it's too early to do that.

This sounds good. Is 1.4 a realistic target?

We have a very real use case. We train machine learning models, then make predictions from them. The former is a batch workload that can and probably always should use a GPU device exclusively, so it's not super interesting for oversubscription. The latter is demand-driven, depending on two main variables: model size and customers' access patterns. We can't give a full, expensive GPU to a model that is very small or has little traffic. Not only it'd be a waste of devices, but, being on EC2, it'd also require many more machines, thus forcing the procurement of a lot of CPU/RAM that aren't actually needed.

Thus, we manually oversubscribe the GPUs (NVIDIA's let you open the same device from multiple processes). We've been doing that in VMs and we've started to do that with Kubernetes, too. Since we have fairly stable GPU memory footprints for each model, we use that to decide how to "pack" things together. That's still "easy" while we only have one hardware vintage for serving. ## kubectl cp fails on large files 

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST?**:

/kind bug

**What happened**:
Copying either a large file, or a large directory from a container via `kubectl cp` results in the error of `error: unexpected EOF` and a failure to transfer.  In my case, the file is 1.7G.

I executed the following command

```bash
kubectl cp infra-cassandra-global-0:cassandra.tar.gz infra-cassandra-global-0-cassandra.tar.gz
```

The command executes, however the terminal will print the error below after 10-14 seconds of execution, and no file is copied. 

```bash
error: unexpected EOF
```


**What you expected to happen**:

The large file to be downloaded from the container.

**How to reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)**:

Add a large file >= 1.7G to any location in the pod.  I was able to re-create this will the file on a PV, or locally on the image file system. 

Execute `kubectl cp` to download the large file.  It will fail.

**Anything else we need to know?**:

**Environment**:
- Kubernetes version (use `kubectl version`): v1.8.6, client v1.8.5
- Cloud provider or hardware configuration: AWS
- OS (e.g. from /etc/os-release): k8s-1.8-debian-jessie-amd64-hvm-ebs-2017-12-02 (ami-06a57e7e)
- Kernel (e.g. `uname -a`):
- Install tools: Kops
- Others:
assistant:
This problem may be solved if kubectl cp would implement resumable downloads to work around temporary network errors.

---

I found the issue here in our environment.  We had blocked ICMP packets in the SG attached to the ENI of our API ELB (CLB these days).  This meant that requests to fragment large packets were not getting back to the ELB.  Because of that, the packet was never re-sent by the ELB, which meant it was lost.  This breaks the TCP session and the connection resets.

In short, make sure that ICMP is allowed between your Load Balancer and your hosts, and your MTU settings are correctly calibrated.

---

Any update on this one?

`unexpected EOF` -> copy and exec outputs
`Error proxying data from client to backend: unexpected EOF` -> API-server
`error forwarding port <port> to pod <pod_id>, uid : EOF:` -> Kubelet

## Excessive conntrack cleanup causes high memory (12GB) and CPU usage when any Pod with a UDP port changes

### What happened?

We are encountering a severe performance issue in kube-proxy (v1.32) when any Pod with a UDP port is updated (e.g., CoreDNS). In the new kube-proxy implementation, changes to Services or Pods that expose UDP ports trigger a full conntrack cleanup. This cleanup process iterates over the entire conntrack table, leading to extremely high resource consumption—sometimes up to 12 GB of memory and 1.5 CPU cores per kube-proxy instance.

In a simple test, we observed 2,780 instances of the log message "Adding conntrack filter for cleanup", which caused an OOM when kube-proxy was limited to 256 MB of memory. Without that limit, kube-proxy memory usage spiked to 12 GB. On nodes with large conntrack tables, kube-proxy effectively becomes stuck, consuming all available memory each time there is a UDP endpoint change.

This issue appears to be systemic; every change in a Pod with a UDP port triggers all kube-proxy instances to perform the extensive cleanup. Currently, there is no option to disable or throttle this behavior, which disrupts cluster stability and can lead to service degradation or outages. We request that the cleanup logic be revised to target only the relevant conntrack entries or that a mechanism be provided to disable or limit this aggressive cleanup behavior.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/127318
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/126130

### What did you expect to happen?

We expected kube-proxy to handle conntrack cleanu
[truncated]

Also I've attached some flame graphs for those not so inclined: [before_after_cpuprofiles.zip](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/files/9224155/before_after_cpuprofiles.zip) k8s at e6a60c3b4d941da805d7e5548ffd462e5babc74d (kubectl timed out applying the cr) after applying #111557 the `mergingWalker` no longer makes an appearance in any cpu samples (and the kubectl apply operation finishes very quickly) --- Okay, I'll let @apelisse confirm, but this has the hallmarks of an issue discovered recently. I'll try to describe it: When a CRD uses `preserveUnknownFields: true` in the OpenAPIv3 schema, Server Side Apply refers to the contents as a "deduced type". There is an issue where when some operations are performed on a deduced type, Server Side Apply performs a compare of the custom resource against itself recursively which is an O(2^n) operation where n is the levels of nesting in custom resource (IIRC the relevant code is https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/structured-merge-diff/blob/65611e5f7fc60e909529889c29faa9cac17cb485/typed/merge.go#L81-L82). The CRD in this example has around 20 levels of nesting, which seems like enough to trigger this issue. The most obvious mitigation that comes to mind to me: Instead of `preserveUnknownFields: true` fully declare the schema of the custom resource using a [structural schema](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/extend-kubernetes/custom-resources/custom-resource-definitions/). And to confirm- This is absolutely a bug that can and should be fixed. --- > Did you see anything in the apiserver logs that might be related to the CPU utilization? We initially noticed the issue on apiserver with a cpu limit of `1` core. In this case the Pod was constantly CPU throttled and a lot of requests were failing sporadically (as expected). ## Add Lazy Evaluation of Resource Quotas Limits

Moving a thread from sig-scheduling slack to here.

**What would you like to be added**:

Some Resource Quota limits should be evaluated lazily in the scheduler instead of eagerly during pod submission. For example, if a namespace RQ limits a user to 1 CPU, that user should be able to submit N number of 1 CPU Pods and they should execute one at a time.  With current implementation any requests beyond the initial 1 CPU Pod will be immediately rejected (i.e. eager evaluation of the quota).

Limits on CPU, RAM, and extended resources such as GPU are examples of quotas which should have a "soft" limit which is evaluated lazily and enforced by the scheduler

Limits on number of items (such as number of Pods/ConfigMaps/etc.) are example of quotas which should probably be evaluated eagerly

**Why is this needed**:

From an end users perspective; if I submit a Pod and the cluster is saturated or my Pod is unschedualable for any other reason my request is accepted and held in the queue for scheduling. However, if my namespace is saturated due to RQs or my request is unscheduable because my request is larger than my quota, my request is rejected immediately. These behaviors are inconsistent for the end user.

In the run-to-completion world, this is a common issue. People do not use Pods, as in the example but they use Jobs or some other type of controller to manage their Pods. Because of the inconsistent behavior each of these controller implementations need to be _Quot
[truncated]

## Horizontal Pod Autoscaler fooled by terminating pods

**What would you like to be added**:

An option inside HPA to ignore "Terminating" pods (i.e. pods that have been elected for downscale but are for example waiting for the prestop hook to give permission to k8s to actually stop them) when computing resource usage.

**Why is this needed**:

Let's take an example.

I have a service S processing some short to long queries (for this example, think 1min in general, but with some queries taking by nature up to 30 min). Each query uses about 5% of CPU. We configure 75% utilisation of the cluster (so an average of 15 queries running on each instance).
_To make this example simpler lets assume that the service doing nothing takes 0% and that we start with only one instance of the service_

When we start the 16th concurrent query, CPU of first instance will be 80% -> HPA will start a second one.
If we keep running 16-30 queries for some time, we will end up having 2 evenly loaded instances with 8-15 queries each. So far so good.

But if for some time we go down to 12 queries, both instances will be at 30% then a downscale will happen and one instance will be "terminating" (and thanks to the prestop hook, it will stay in that state until the last query ends, so up to 30 min based on our initial assumption of the duration of queries). And thanks to Murphy's law, the instance elected for termination was running 5 short queries that end quickly and one long that was started just before the downscale and that will last for 30 
[truncated]
assistant:
This caused an outage for us.
We had 6 running webserver pods serving our website and then we performed a deployment.
6 more new pods came up, and the 6 old pods went into a Terminating state, all of which were there for several minutes due to the preStop lifecycle rules.
During this time, the HPA was including the terminating pods in its CPU calculations and decided it would scale down the new webservers by half reducing the 6 new pods to 3.
The 3 remaining webservers were quickly overloaded and an outage ensued.

## QoS tiers

We have described QoS tiers in several presentations and the necessary underlying mechanisms are supported by lmctfy:
http://www.slideshare.net/dotCloud/eric-brewer-dockercon-keynote
http://www.slideshare.net/vmarmol/containers-google
http://www.linuxplumbersconf.org/2011/ocw/system/presentations/705/original/Control_Loops_In_Userspace.pdf
https://github.com/google/lmctfy

Borg: https://research.google.com/pubs/pub43438.html

We should expose QoS tiers through the Kubernetes pod API.
assistant:
Caveat, still coming up to speed on everything, but that won't stop me from
having an opinion.

QoS and administrative or config information should never be labels.
 Labels are the domain of the user, not the system.

On Fri, Jun 20, 2014 at 7:50 AM, Clayton Coleman notifications@github.com
wrote:

> Would QoS tiers specified via labeling or orthogonal to labeling (assuming
> orthogonal given presentations, just want to make sure)? Do you see the
> need for feedback between a minion about current resource load and the
> scheduler, and if so, is this a backchannel (a formal event bus) concept
> that is part of Kubernetes or encapsulated behind the scheduler interface?
> 
> —
> Reply to this email directly or view it on GitHub
> https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/kubernetes/issues/147#issuecomment-46686825
> .

---

Current thinking for basic tiers:
- Support specification of limit (max allowed) and request (min required for scheduling)
- If limit==request, then it's highest priority
- If limit > request, then middle priority (main reason to need this is for vertical auto-sizing)
- If no limit or request, then lowest priority
- Suggested defaulting is described in [resources.md](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/kubernetes/blob/master/docs/resources.md)
- High-priority pods/containers would get reserved resources.
- Lower-priority pods/containers would be put into cgroups with the resources reserved by the higher-priority ones subtracted, in addition to nested cgroups with their specified limits (using the proposed parent cgroup Docker feature)
- Containers specifying no cpu would get a tiny number of shares

We could also use core allocation, oom score adjustment, and other mechanisms to strengthen this basic protection.

---

@smarterclayton : I agree with @thockin . QoS specifications need to be first-class fields in our API, together with resource specifications (#168).

For one possible example of what this could look like, see lmctfy:
https://github.com/google/lmctfy/blob/master/include/lmctfy.proto

## Tune APF list work estimator for large responses

## What would you like to be added?

The API Priority and Fairness (APF) mechanism effectively protects the Kubernetes API server from CPU-bound overloads. However, it currently falls short in preventing memory exhaustion, particularly from large LIST responses. The size of LIST responses can vary drastically, ranging from a few kilobytes to hundreds of megabytes. APF's current cost estimation for requests does not adequately account for the memory footprint of these responses. This oversight can lead to API server memory exhaustion (OOMs), reduced stability, and inefficient resource utilization.

Goals
* Reduce the maximum memory usage of API server without impact on throughput.

Non-Goals
* Model non linear relations in memory usage, this should be reserved for future where we might use a proper model.
* Load shedding that happens when cost of upcoming request eshauses CPU causing GC to starve and leak memory.

## Proposal

To mitigate memory overloads and prevent OOMs, I propose to incorporate the object size as a primary signal into the APF LIST work estimator. This will allow APF to better reflect the memory cost of these requests and allocate "seats" proportionally, as based on experiments in most cases it is a linear relation.

Specifically, I propose the following changes to the LIST work estimator:
* Replace the existing object count estimator with a size-based model:
  * Requests served from etcd: Assign 1 seat per 100 KB of objects response size. 
  * Requests serv
[truncated]

Broadly speaking, I am sympathetic to the concerns in this Issue. I see two.

1. The technique for assigning width to a LIST request looks only at object count, not result size (in bytes). The latter makes a big difference in serving cost.
2. The width of a request is capped at 10 (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/0017db180b40bfb0db6097fa4e0f46dd8eae4a84/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/flowcontrol/request/config.go#L27). This does not allow enough range to correspond to the range of result sizes.

For concern 1, I agree that the question is, can this be estimated with useful accuracy in a way that is inexpensive enough. This estimation is an overhead on each LIST request. I will defer to storage experts on this question. We could do experiments on PRs; the Prometheus metrics include `apiserver_request_filter_duration_seconds`.

For concern 2, we have to remember that when a request's width equals or exceeds a priority level's concurrency allocation, APF allows just one request at a time (in that level). [truncated]

The technique is indirect. APF can not hope to monitor all relevant resources. Some of them are remote. Some of them are not instrumented. APF relies on administrative configuration of the concurrency limit to be low enough.

I think I ran into this situation as well. It was caused by a similar situation as described by @seeruk . A change to a Terraform module resulted in ASG replacement. The change was supposed to be deployed by hand on each ASG individually, which though not as graceful as using taints to drain the nodes in the old ASG, would have probably allowed Kubernetes to reschedule the unexpectedly terminated pods without too much difficulty. Instead, the change was inadvertently applied to a large number of ASGs all at roughly the same time. This caused many ReplicaSets to report 0 current and 0 ready pods, and to have the "Error creating: the server was unable to return a response in the time allotted, but may still be processing the request (post pods)" event.

Luckily, manually scaling each ReplicaSet to 0 and then back to the original scale gave Kubernetes the kick it needed to create the pods.

## ProgressDeadlineExceeded not set outside of Deployment rollouts

I'd floated #93933 earlier with a PR attempting to address this, and more recently opened [rhbz#1983823][1].  Here's a third try at pitching this proposal 🤞.

/sig apps

#### Proposal

When a Deployment has a single underlying ReplicaSet that had previously completed, but which now lacks the target count of ready replicas, and the underling ReplicaSet fails to make progress for more than `progressDeadlineSeconds`, the Deployment controller should set `Progressing=False` with `ProgressDeadlineExceeded`.

##### Benefits

Deployment owners get a clear signal that the Deployment controller (via the delegated ReplicaSet controller) is struggling to recover the desired state.  In situations where the Deployment controller continues to be unable to make progress, additional disruption will eventually push us into `Available=False`.  But `Progressing=False` with `ProgressDeadlineExceeded` is a way the Deployment controller can request assistance before things get bad enough to go `Available=False`.

##### Downsides

Maybe someone depends on the current behavior and would be broken by the proposed pivot.  Feedback welcome, if anyone can think of a use case that might be vulnerable.

#### Context

The only `Progressing` condition in kubernetes/kubernetes is `DeploymentProgressing`, so we don't have to worry about changes to the Deployment controller's handling being inconsistent with other core controllers.

The dev-facing godocs for Deployment's `Progressing` [is][
[truncated]
assistant:
For now I can see one issue with inconsistency of the Progressing condition ( I want to dive into the impact of this feature more later).

meaning of the old behaviour:
- `Progressing=True` with `NewReplicaSetAvailable` with all available pods is saying: we know this rollout has an ability to progress and we have 100% availability
- `Progressing=True` with `NewReplicaSetAvailable` with unavailable pods is saying: we know this rollout has an ability to progress even though we are not on 100% availability anymore
- `Progressing=False` with `ProgressDeadlineExceeded` is saying: we  know this rollout does not have an ability to progress

meaning of the new behaviour:
- `Progressing=True` with `NewReplicaSetAvailable` with all available pods : we know this rollout has an ability to progress and we have 100% availability
- `Progressing=False` with `ProgressDeadlineExceeded` is saying: we do not know if this rollout has an ability to progress (it might) and some pods are unavailable

So the difference with the new behaviour is that we cannot infer if we have a healthy rollout when some pods are disrupted but we have a better signal that the pods are disrupted.

Also in either
[truncated]

---

One thing to note is that since this is fixing a rare behaviour (deployment pods are disrupted after successful rollout), the things this would break would also be rare.

For example. it could lead to race conditions in consumers which are polling the deployment status.

1. poller checks if rollout done
2. rollout completes
3. pod is disrupted -> rollout waiting for deployment
4. poller checks if rollout done
5. rollout waiting for deployment
6. 4. and 5. lasts for some amount of time
7. rollout exceeded its progress deadline and sets ProgressDeadlineExceeded
8. poller times out with ProgressDeadlineExceeded
9. rollout completes and the disruption dissapears
10 poller couldn't execute the logic after succesfull rollout as it could before


You can take a look at this case here: [status_check.go#L308](https://github.com/GoogleContainerTools/skaffold/blob/fc060164fcb42d7d648df5ed5125571fde572e3f/pkg/skaffold/kubernetes/status/status_check.go#L308) & [status_check.go#L220](https://github.com/GoogleContainerTools/skaffold/blob/fc060164fcb42d7d648df5ed5125571fde572e3f/pkg/skaffold/kubernetes/status/status_check.go#L220)

> The Kubernetes provider for Terraform treats P

In situations where the Deployment controller continues to be unable to make progress, additional disruption will eventually push us into Available=False. But Progressing=False with ProgressDeadlineExceeded is a way the Deployment controller can request assistance before things get bad enough to go Available=False.

So to reitarate, the issue is, to have an option as a cluster admin to look at any Deployment to see  if any of its replicas lost Availability. And to have easier time reading disrupted/misbehaving deployments.

## Monitoring and managing the quota size of containers and local volumes

Today no process on the node manages the amount of storage in use for:
- Container CoW layers (the container's writable directory)
- Empty dir volumes
- Docker volumes created implicitly on startup of the container

Since neither Docker nor the host operating system can totally manage this, I think it should be possible to have a management loop in / around the kubelet to control the total usage of these elements.  Today, it's possible to write to the empty dir until you fill the filesystem, at which point all pods fail.  You _could_ set quotas (if the uids of the processes are different), but that requires all your pods to be running unique uids (or set quota to a multiple of the count of the pods with the same UID which doesn't protect against bad actors).

I would propose the following
- Add something to cAdvisor to track top layer CoW usage where possible for the filesystems that can provide it 
  - Docker today does not expose this - we could potentially add an abstraction endpoint there, or do the checks directly against devmapper / overlay
- In the Kubelet, honor a resource limit for usage of emptyDir (per mount / pod?) as well as the cow filesystem with a simple check loop.
  - In situations where the container CoW layer has exceeded the maximum, restart the container gracefully (this is hard to do outside of the kubelet today)
  - In situations where the emptyDir has exceeded size... do something??
- Report the volume and cow metrics up through cAdvisor
- Support rea
[truncated]
assistant:
Resources on EmptyDir volumes: SGTM. Use ResourceRequirements (requests and limits), exactly like PersistentVolumeClaimSpec:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/api/v1/types.go#L453
but actually support limits as well as requests.

I've heard that not all union filesystem implementations support quota and/or have problems with the implementations. Would love to collapse filesystem layers into readonly image plus CoW layer, though that wasn't on your list.

Storage resources on containers would apply to container-related disk usage: writable layer and (ideally) logs.

I don't object to optionally readonly filesystems. It should be handled exactly like SecurityContext.

We've planned to use supplemental gids to track local storage usage for a long time now.

---

Just an idea: what about a new volume plugin, which would create a (sparse) file of a given size on the host, create a filesystem on it and use it instead of emptyDir? That would enforce the quota in pretty hard way, with very little overhead in the OS and without periodical `du -s` polling. In addition, the container would get nice ENOSPC errors when the inner or the outer filesystems gets full, no special actions would be needed from Kubernetes side.

As downside, when the volume is heavily used and data is being deleted from the volume, they would still consume data in the host (outer) filesystem. Periodic fstrim could help here.

And of course, this does not solve container CoW layer quota.

---

- cAdvisor tracks filesystem usage for containers if AUFS driver is enabled. Relevant code [here](https://github.com/google/cadvisor/blob/master/container/docker/handler.go#L262).
- cAdvisor ignores other volumes mounted into a container since it does not understand volume sharing semantics. 
- The existing logic can be used for tracking usage of emptyDir volumes.
- One thing to consider is that tracking filesystem usage without quota can be pretty expensive, especially when dealing with deep directories. 
- I really like the read-only filesystem option, but @bgrant0607 had reservations against that. Ideally, I want that to be the default policy. 

How about using quota with supplemental Gids? I was planning on posting a proposal for disk management by end of this week. Would it be ok to discuss disk management using that proposal?

## Total amount of ephemeral-storage in namespace is not limited/tracked properly

### What happened?

In ResourceQuota's doc( https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/resource-quotas/ )m written as:

- A resource quota, defined by a ResourceQuota object, provides constraints that limit aggregate resource consumption per namespace. It can limit the quantity of objects that can be created in a namespace by type, as well as the total amount of compute resources that may be consumed by resources in that namespace.
- Users create resources (pods, services, etc.) in the namespace, and the quota system tracks usage to ensure it does not exceed hard resource limits defined in a ResourceQuota.

But actually, total amount of ephemeral-storage in namespace is not limited/tracked properly.
Because, we can create pods which don't have limit.ephemeral-storage.
When I don't set limits.cpu/memory in pod and limits.cpu/memory is set in ResourceQuota, I get the following error.

	// let's not make that mistake again with other resources now that QoS is defined.

> 
> The current `ResourceQuota` API object does not allow the ability
> to quota best-effort pods separately from pods with resource guarantees.
> For example, if a cluster-admin applies a quota that caps requested
> cpu at 10 cores and memory at 10Gi, all pods in the namespace must
> make an explicit resource request for cpu and memory to satisfy
> quota.  This prevents a namespace with a quota from supporting best-effort
> pods.
> 
> In practice, the cluster-admin wants to control the impact of best-effort
> pods to the cluster, but not restrict the ability to run best-effort pods
> altogether.
> 
> As a result, the cluster-admin requires the ability to control the
> max number of active best-effort pods.  In addition, the cluster-admin
> requires the ability to scope a quota that limits compute resources to
> exclude best-effort pods.

## NodePort quota also disallows services of type ClusterIP

**What happened**:
We deployed resource quotas to disallow services of type NodePort. Some teams had already deployed services with NodePorts/LoadBalancer. So they are above their limit. Now if we try to deploy a service of type ClusterIP, this is forbidden, although that is not restricted by resource quotas.

**What you expected to happen**:
I expect for a resource quota for NodePorts to only disallow NodePorts and have no influence on other services.

**How to reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)**:
Apply the following file:
[long code block omitted]

CLI Output is then:
[long code block omitted]
- Cloud provider or hardware configuration: Tested on local setup (Vagrant). Discovered with older Kubernetes version on AWS
- OS (e.g. from /etc/os-release): Ubuntu respectively CoreOS
- Install tools: kubeadm respectively kops
assistant:
alright, normally the resource quota for NodePorts do *not* limit the number of services for ClusterIP, it only happens when the resource quota for NodePorts have already been *exceeded*. Put it another way, if NodePorts quota is 1, and its usage is 2, then no more services are allowed to be created, no matter what type of services they are.

On the other hand, if the NodePorts quota is 1, and its usage is 1, then it is totally ok to create another service of type ClusterIP (of course, it does not allow to create another service of type NodePort, as expected)

user:
## DRA: structured parameters: management containers

### What would you like to be added?

Management containers are contains that get access to some hardware while it is in use by some workload.

In terms of the "named resources" structured model that means that the resource instance is not considered "allocated" when a claim for such a management container uses it.

/cc @johnbelamaric @klueska 
/sig node
/triage accepted
/priority important-longterm

We would like to support this in Kubernetes 1.31, but can proceed without it.

### Why is this needed?

Mostly monitoring.
assistant:
Allowing management access to in-use resources is (potentially) a privileged operation. I can see two ways to achieve this:

We add a "management" boolean flag to a resource class. That flag applies to all claims using it. We then use the [pending quota PR](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/114916) to limit access to this class to specific namespaces. When quota is exceeded (i.e. access is disabled), creating claims is prevented.

We add a "management" boolean flag to ResourceClaimParameters. Then a single class can be used for normal claims and management. To limit usage of that flag, we need a quota mechanism that considers resource claim parameters. This implies that this needs to be implemented as part of allocation during scheduling.

---

We support this currently (with opaque paramters) in the https://github.com/NVIDIA/k8s-dra-driver using a namespace annotation. Only namespaces that have this annotation are allowed to request resources in this way (using a dedicated field in our standard GpuClaimParamters object). This only works, however, because our custom controller is able to enforce this (which is not possible in the new structured parameters way of doing things).

For structured parameters, I don't necessarily have a preference on which of the two approaches you mention are followed, so long as the cluster admin is still able to pick-and-choose which namespaces are allowed to request resources in this way.

---

This is implemented in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/125488 with access control of the feature via validating admission policy. From Slack:
[long code block omitted]

> It looks like creating a ResourceQuota object is not having an effect immediately, causing a race that (very rarely!) goes wrong.

This occurs *after* the test has ensured that the apiserver has applied the updated quota. It does that by checking the status of the ResourceQuota for `hard: 1, limit: 1`.

The expectation is that once the apiserver has activated a quota, it enforces it 100% reliably during the admission phase for a new resource. If that isn't true, then users could create more than they are allowed by exploiting this race.

---

> Is it more likely this is a bug somewhere in between ResourceQuota and the API server than with ResourceClaims?

assistant:
I had this same issue, and it turned out `SystemdCgroup` wasn't getting set properly in `/etc/containerd/config.toml`. I was only using the configuration in the [container runtime installation docs](https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/production-environment/container-runtimes/#containerd-systemd); I didn't have the rest of the config file. After generating a baseline config with `containerd config default > /etc/containerd/config.toml` and editing the `SystemdCgroup` setting there, the problem went away.

(The docs mention this in a note, but mostly qualifies it as something to try if you used a distro package, which I didn't do, so I had skipped over it.)

## Exceeded quota for pod priority class

**What happened**:
Quota was exceeded for a pod priority class.

**What you expected to happen**:
Quota is enforced for a pod priority class.

**How to reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)**:

1. Create a PriorityClass.
2. Create a pod using the PriorityClass created in step 1 and wait for it to be Running.
3. Create a ResourceQuota with a hard limit of 2 pods for the PriorityClass created in step 1.
4. Create a pod using the PriorityClass created in step 1 and wait for it to be Running.
5. Create one final pod using the PriorityClass created in step 1.  This should fail and it normally does.  However, there appears to be a timing issue that sometimes allows the pod to be created.

**Anything else we need to know?**:
No.

**Environment**:
- Kubernetes version (use `kubectl version`): 1.12.5
- Cloud provider or hardware configuration: IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service
- OS (e.g. from /etc/os-release): `Ubuntu 16.04.5 LTS`
- Kernel (e.g. `uname -a`): `Linux 4.4.0-141-generic`
- Install tools: Installed via managed service
- Others: N/A
assistant:
I cannot reproduce this on a v1.20.2 Kind cluster, but I tried to look into the source code:

1. It appears controller-manager is responsible for updating the ResourceQuota's usage.
2. The Quota admission logic relies on reading the RQs from a [rqAccessor(lister)](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/9e5fcc49ec568f222a5274f443903d89fec3e591/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/plugin/resourcequota/controller.go#L179). For each RQ get from RQLister, it's compared to a local LRU cache and returned.

So, if there is a bug, maybe it hides in the logic that QuotaAdmission holds a stale quota object whose status.used is not updated (by controller-manager) yet?

BTW: It's more an area in api-machinery, so

/sig api-machinery

## Deprecate PodNodeSelector admission plugin

### What would you like to be added?

Deprecate PodNodeSelector admission plugin and the associated annotation `scheduler.alpha.kubernetes.io/node-selector`

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/plugin/pkg/admission/podnodeselector/admission.go

### Why is this needed?

This plugin has been in alpha for a long time and never graduated. There are other ways now to support a similar behavior using mutating webhooks.

/sig scheduling
assistant:
Yes, they're all in alpha level, but that's may because no one is following these two features as it behaves well, but doesn't mean no end-users. 

Our product relies on these two plugins to force pods with-in the same namespace running on the specified nodes, it's like a hard constraint. And this feature is required by several of our customers, so from the users POV, we hope to keep them as they are.  

Or do you think we should get some feedbacks from the community, email survey etc.?

---

> If we deprecate something, it's not removed immediately. Deprecation means “you should stop using this feature”. When we assert that, you should stop. The deprecation lets us make sure that Kubernetes overall is well maintained, and that's a quality that our end users value.

For alpha level features, we can remove them directly https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/using-api/deprecation-policy/#deprecation

But considering this is a feature came to reality a long time ago, adding a warning before removal may not surprise end-users a lot. 

> There are other ways now to support a similar behavior using mutating webhooks (stable), or perhaps via [scheduling plugins](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/scheduling-eviction/scheduling-framework/).

If we have one approach in kubernetes that meets users' requirements, why should we remove them instead asking the users to implement themselves, which we say reimplementing the wheels. And I don't think scheduler-plugins can achieve this, it's more about scheduling.

> (If you're relying on an alpha feature of Kubernetes, that's absolutely your prerogative, but as a project I'm confident that we've made the story clear. Alpha lev
[truncated]

---

If I do a search on open/closed issues with keyword `PodNodeSelector` in this repository, people are suggested to use PodNodeSelector as a namespace scoped node selector. And then I see this threat intending to remove this feature. 

I am a bit confused what would be the suggested approach now. Namespace level node isolation is a legit use case for K8s administrator, when you don't have much control on a pod template inside the namespace while you still want to enforce pods not being scheduled in unintended nodes. Even with taint/toleration applied an end users can still bypass the security boundary to schedule the pod in a privileged node by adding toleration if they know the target taint. 

I did a some research and seems the only alternative which is not in alpha is to use Dynamic Admission Control and write our own piece of Admission Webhook. Since a lot of people are relying on this feature, why not moving it to GA?

user:
## Resource Quotas: Possibility to not enforce limits.cpu per pod

### What would you like to be added?

Currently, for cpu (and memory) resources, ResourceQuotas enforce that every (new) pod in that namespace sets a limit for that resource.

There should be a new option for the limits.cpu ResourceQuota at namespace level:
`enforceLimitPerPod: true (default) / false`

If false, it's optional to define limits.cpu for each pod in this namespace. `limits.cpu` of the namespace minus `limits.cpu` of each pod (where defined) should be used to limit CPU usage across all other pods in this namespace.

### Why is this needed?

According to https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/resource-quotas/,
you can limit the total sum of compute resources that can be requested in a given namespace. limits.cpu for the namespace ist computed as follows: Across all pods in a non-terminal state, the sum of CPU limits cannot exceed this value.

This is a problem with workloads which have a low base CPU usage with short cpu spikes randomly distributed across pods and time. A lot of assigned CPU is wasted in such case.
For such workload scenarios, it would make more sense to limit only the sum of CPU usage of all pods in the namespace instead of limiting each pod separately.

Example: 
limits.cpu namespace: 8
16 containers with limits.cpu 500m

Each of these containers are limited to 500m CPU which could affect application performance negatively.  If specification of limits.cpu is not enforced in this namespace, each pod could use up to 7.985 C
[truncated]
assistant:
> He wants to limit the CPU usage of the set of all pods in the namespace to 8, which should be possible via cgroup.

cgroups are local to the node running the pod, but pods in a namespace are not all on the same node. That means real time CPU usage would have to be reported back into the API, observed by other nodes, and cgroup rules updated in response, at high frequency, for this to work. You need some indicator on each pod how the CPU limits should be set to inform the node how to set its cgroups.

---
