user:
## Various performance issues

There seems to be significant performance issues, some visible effects are slow reconcile times, and loading the dashboard being incredibly slow when there are many resources. I will go into the details on what I think the issues are, and how they could potentially be fixed. Once the issues are addressed, I am expecting a large increase in performance.

One issue is that the underlying function GetAllTopControllers in controller-utils is very slow, because it has to make a lot of API calls. This function is used in two places that can both be avoided. It is used during reconciliation to get the list of controllers, for which we simply fetch a list of well-known controllers (CronJob, DaemonSet, Deployment, Job, StatefulSet) that can be changed through CLI configuration. The function is also used to get the current resource requests and limits for the dashboard, which can be avoided by either referencing the target defined in VPA directly, or again fetching the list of controllers.

Another issue has to do with reconciliation logic. A namespace reconcile currently triggers when the namespace changes or when a pod inside the namespace changes. However, an initial update event is sent for every pod and namespace on controller startup, which means each namespace will be reconciled many many times. On first thought, I am unsure why it is necessary to reconcile on pod change. I thought it would be sufficient for pod create and delete, or better, controller create and delete.


[truncated]
assistant:
👋🏼  Sorry for the slow response. We have definitely been seeing some of these errors in other places that use controller-utils. Robert just made some fixes to it that should help with performance, so I'm hoping we can get those in and see if it helps. Once that's done, perhaps we can continue this thread to see if there are other opportunities. Thanks!

---

https://github.com/FairwindsOps/controller-utils/blob/v0.3.2/pkg/controller/controller.go#L136-L155


This generates an n^2 amount of load on API server, with n being number of pods in each cluster namespace.

user:
## Support additional properties on VPAs like ResourcePolicy

It would be great if there was some additional labels available that could configure certain VPA features such as the ResourcePolicy for the VPAs

A resource policy contains container policies that allow you to exclude/include specific containers from the updateMode, set min/max allowed cpu and memory.  They also allow setting the controlledValues property which can tell the VPAs to set requests and limits (RequestsAndLimits) or requests only (RequestOnly)

These can all be very helpful when needing to have more flexibility 

Some good reference articles

https://povilasv.me/vertical-pod-autoscaling-the-definitive-guide/
https://betterprogramming.pub/understanding-vertical-pod-autoscaling-in-kubernetes-6d53e6d96ef3

I would be happy to work on adding this - if this is appropriate for this project.

Thank you!

## Support for Jobs

As with #84 and #96 it would be really helpful for us to have support for pods created from Job resources. We use Jobs to run background workers that autoscale as the queues get loaded and clear. 

The workers are [generated based on queue hooks](https://github.com/keylimetoolbox/resque-kubernetes) and in our implementation have unique names for each job. So I'm looking for something more complicated that the current deployments which provide metrics based on the `metadata.name`. I'd like to aggregate resource recommendations based on jobs with the same `metadata.labels` values.

This might not be possible at this time, because it doesn't look like the [Job resource](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.13/#job-v1-batch) has a Scale subresource as [required for the `targetRef`](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/verticalpodautoscaler#verticalpodautoscalerspec_v1_autoscalingk8sio).

Any thoughts on whether this is possible or other approaches to get this?

## Empty recommendations for operator with scale subresource 

### What happened?

Hello,

some containers in goldilocks UI are empty (no recommendations displayed)

After some investigation, it seems we face the issue as described in this VPA ticket : https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/issues/5836

Summary for convenience:

We use EMQX operator https://github.com/emqx/emqx-operator
It introduces a CRD called "EMQX" which has a scale subresource
When you create an "kind: EMQX" resource, the operator then creates a statefulset, which also has a scale subresource
and the result is the VPA description has the same error :
```
The targetRef controller has a parent but it should point to a topmost well-known or scalable controller
```

As the VPA is created by goldilocks, I was asked to also open an issue here.

### What did you expect to happen?

Goldilocks is able to display recommendations for any kind of workloads, whatever the controller responsible for it.

### How can we reproduce this?

Annotate a namespace with goldilocks annotation, deploy an operator that manages Custom resources with a scale subresource (like EMQX operator for example) and check that VPA has an error about the controller targetRef and that goldilocks does not display any information about the workload.

### Version

4.8.3

### Search

- [X] I did search for other open and closed issues before opening this.

### Code of Conduct

- [X] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

### Additional context

_No response_
assistant:
The same happens to me with Prometheus, Alertmanager and some Longhorn resources and the issue is exactly as described. Goldilock goes up the set of resources and find the top most that is not supported by VPA.
For example, for prometheus pod it finds the related STS, that is created by a Prometheus Custom Resource, the VPA is created as it follows
```
apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1
kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  labels:
    creator: Fairwinds
    source: goldilocks
  name: goldilocks-promstack-prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  targetRef:
    apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
    kind: Prometheus
    name: promstack-prometheus
  updatePolicy:
    updateMode: "Off"
 ```
note that`spec.targetRef.kind` is `Prometheus` while it should be `StatefulSet`.

This issue seems to be related to https://github.com/FairwindsOps/goldilocks/issues/500 that has been closed in October 2022, with no apparent solution. I checked https://github.com/fairwindsops/controller-utils but I could not find any mention of the current issue

---

Thanks for the report, we'll have to work on reproducing this and see what's up with our logic that finds the top-level controller

---

> @yogeek you likely need to add RBAC to grant Goldilocks access to those resources. If you're deploying via Helm, you can use `.controller.rbac.extraRules`

Actually that does not help, that will allow Goldilocks to track the top objects, but those are not supported by VPA, so a VPA definition will indeed be created, but it will remain empty.

user:
## VPA TargetRef created can be considered invalid by K8s Autoscaler Recommender

### What happened?

Goldilocks controller walks a chain of owner references and uses the TopController as the Target Reference in the VPA it creates.

The logic in k8s vertical-pod-autoscaler recommender requires the target ref to pass a ["isWellKnownOrScalable"](https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/blob/master/vertical-pod-autoscaler/pkg/recommender/input/controller_fetcher/controller_fetcher.go#L256) test.

There are circumstances where the VPAs created by the Goldilocks controller do not pass that test. One example is via PrometheusOperator, an Alertmanager resource creating a Statefulset and then Pods. The target reference specified by Goldilocks is ```monitoring.coreos.com/v1``` ```Alertmanager``` ```prom-operator-alertmanager```. This passes neither the Wellknown nor the Scalable tests and so receives no recommendations and shows as empty in the Goldilocks UI.
If i manually adjust the target reference configuration to be the Alertmanager child stateful set, the VPA receives recommendations (target reference is well known).

There should be additional logic in determining the TopController that considers the acceptance criteria specified by the K8s VPA Recommender.

Perhaps a check for Scalable can be incorporated and a default fall back to "well known" implemented ? 

Otherwise a flag to prefer well known would go a long way to covering usage patterns that are currently not handled in a way that works with the Recommender.

### What did you expect to 
[truncated]

We discussed a bit internally and I think it makes sense to add it there, but we want to do it in a way that's a bit more flexible, perhaps accepting a function to modify the behavior.

@JaimeHolland - I managed to workaround your error by implementing the following RBAC permissions:
```yaml
controller:
  rbac:
    enableArgoproj: false
    extraRules:
      - verbs:
          - 'get'
          - 'list'
          - 'watch'
        apiGroups:
          - 'argoproj.io'
        resources:
          - '*'
```
However, looks like `Goldilocks` controller still cannot scan the pods. I can the following error in the logs and think it may be related:
```
1 handler.go:41] Object has unknown type of <nil>
```
I think developers need to add the pods controller `Workflow` as an option to Goldilocks Workloads.

## Avoid creating Goldilocks managed VPA if a VPA already exists

If for a deployment object, a VPA already exists before goldilocks installed, would goldilocks create another VPA for this deployment object?

I didn't see this mentioned in the docs, and by reading through the code it seems goldilocks ignores VPAs that doesn't have `VPALabels` in labels, so my understanding is yes goldilocks will create VPAs in this case.

This is a concern for a usecase that some legacy VPAs are already installed in cluster, and goldilocks is installed, and some duplicated VPAs are installed, which is [bad according to vertical-pod-autoscaler known limitations](https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/tree/master/vertical-pod-autoscaler#known-limitations): 
> Multiple VPA resources matching the same pod have undefined behavior.

Furthermore goldilocks should also avoid creating VPAs for deployments that have CPU or memory based HPAs configured:
> Vertical Pod Autoscaler should not be used with the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) on CPU or memory at this moment. However, you can use VPA with HPA on custom and external metrics.

user:
## Do not create VPA for a workload if an HPA or VPA already exists

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
For workloads where HPAs or VPAs are already created, Goldilocks should avoid creating VPAs for them.

If a workload already has a VPA created, currently Goldilocks still creates a VPA for that workload, potentially overwriting the existing resource, or creating a conflicting VPA.

If a workload already has a HPA created, and the HPA works based on CPU or memory, Goldilocks should also avoid creating VPA for it, because it's a [known limitation](https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/tree/master/vertical-pod-autoscaler#known-limitations):
> Vertical Pod Autoscaler should not be used with the [Horizontal Pod Autoscaler](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/horizontal-pod-autoscale/#support-for-resource-metrics) (HPA) on CPU or memory at this moment. However, you can use VPA with [HPA on custom and external metrics](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/horizontal-pod-autoscale/#scaling-on-custom-metrics).

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Goldilocks controller will need to keep track of existing VPAs and HPAs in the cluster, maps them to all discovered workloads, use this information to determine if it should create VPA for each workload.

Additionally, when user creates a conflicting HPA or VPA object, Goldilocks should also cleanup its managed HPA and VPA objects
assistant:
I have a couple questions/concerns about this, and the proposed implementation.

> If a workload already has a VPA created, currently Goldilocks still creates a VPA for that workload, potentially overwriting the existing resource

This is false, unless for some reason you are manually creating VPAs that are prefixed with `goldilocks-`, which would be... odd?

> or creating a conflicting VPA.

Only in the case of using VPAs in non-off mode, which is not the original intended use-case for Goldilocks. 

- Why would an end user enable Goldilocks in a namespace where they are managing their own VPAs? This seems like optimizing for an unintended use-case
- What about the case where a goldilocks managed vpa is in recommend (off) mode, and it is prefixed with goldilocks-. This has no impact to existing VPAs and can provide meaningful information on the dashboard.
- How will this change in behavior be reflected in the dashboard? 
- We are planning to add [this PR](https://github.com/FairwindsOps/goldilocks/pull/641) where the  dashboard provides a warning to the user if they are using an HPA, this will overlap with or eliminate that functionality, and should be considered.


[truncated]

## Recommend < 250Mi for memory request

It seems that the minimum recommended memory request is 250Mi, even if the app is regularly using less than that. How is this calculated? Is it configurable?
assistant:
This is a setting in the vertical pod autoscaler recommender that you can pass as a flag:

```
└─ docker run us.gcr.io/k8s-artifacts-prod/autoscaling/vpa-recommender:0.8.0 --help
pflag: help requested
Usage of /recommender:
...
      --pod-recommendation-min-memory-mb float        Minimum memory recommendation for a pod (default 250)
...
```

---

In this case we recommend you manage the VPA install outside of the Goldilocks install. The chart does not currently support customizing the VPA install.

Since VPA is still relatively "beta" they don't have a great install process in place.  The flag would be set directly in the deployment configuration.

---

My hope is to eventually just write an actual configurable VPA chart and use it as a sub-chart for Goldilocks. Unfortunately that's low on the priority list right now.

assistant:
@huguesalary descheduler evicts pods from overutilized nodes (decided by thresholds) to move those pods to less utilized nodes so that cluster can get more balanced. Since based on your thresholds (50,50,50), all nodes are under threshold, so there is no need to evict any pods.

@huguesalary the [cluster-autoscaler](https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/tree/master/cluster-autoscaler) might work for your use case (better utilizing other nodes and saving money).

> --scale-down-utilization-threshold float                             Sum of cpu or memory of all pods running on the node divided by node's corresponding allocatable resource, below which a node can be considered for scale down (default 0.5)

While cluster-autoscaler is able to concentrate the load in fewer nodes, I'd say that descheduler does the opposite: spread the load among them.

## Use actual node resource utilization in the strategy "LowNodeUtilization"

Currently, pods' request resource requirements are considered for computing node resource utilization in the strategy "LowNodeUtilization", is it more rational to use actual node resource utilization as judgement?

It is common that resource limit of pod is larger than request, and after the scheduling of default scheduler (by resource request), It's probably that cluster is balanced. But the actual resources usage of pod could be much larger than request, which may lead some nodes under pressure.

So is it much more reasonable to combine with metrics server and use actual node resource utilization.
assistant:
@Stefik95 the linked enhancements around real load aware scheduling are still being worked on (mainly in the scheduler-plugins repo, under the "Trimaran" name).

It was mentioned above, but getting actual pod consumption relies on access to the metrics api. To move forward with this, we should look into what we need to be able to access those metrics from within descheduler (and fallbacks/disable when those metrics aren't available). Any help with this step is welcome, it would likely follow a similar pattern to Trimaran's metrics collection.

As a side note, there were also metrics recently added to report the scheduler's "observed" usage based on limits/requests for administrators to compare to real usage (https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/pull/1916 and https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/94866). This is intended to help admins optimize their requests and limits to better reflect actual values.

---

@zhiyxu it looks like you are not the first person to request this feature. See the discussions in #123 , #118, and #7. Based on the discussions in those issues it looks like the descheduler `LowNodeUtilization` strategy is still using requests because this aligns with how the k8s scheduler works. Also, this feature is mentioned in the [roadmap](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler#roadmap).

@damemi @aveshagarwal @ravisantoshgudimetla has anything changed recently to enable the k8s scheduler to use real load metrics during scheduling? For example could the new scheduler framework some how enable this feature in the scheduler? Maybe a custom plugin using the scheduler framework could be created to take real load metrics into account?

---

@zhiyxu and @kangtiann here are my initial thoughts on what the API spec might look like. Please let me know what you think. I'm pretty confident the v1alpha2 `LowNodeUtilization` strategy will need to be adjusted.

I believe it would be a good idea to write a proposal for this and have SIG scheduling review it.

Create a  new v1alpha1 `LowNodeAllocation` strategy. This strategy will work identically to the request based v1alpha `LowNodeUtilization` strategy. The usage of the work allocation is inspired by the discussion in #7. 
[long code block omitted]
apiVersion: "descheduler/v1alpha2".   # Bump to v1alpha2
kind: "DeschedulerPolicy"
strategies:
  "LowNodeUtilization":
     enabled: true
     params:
       nodeResourceUtilizationThresholds:
         thresholds:
           "cpu" : 20
           "memory": 20
         targetThresholds:
           "cpu" : 50
           "memory": 50
```

## The pods evicted by LowNodeUtilization are now placed in the same previous Node.

The pods evicted by LowNodeUtilization are now placed in the same previous Node.
A pod evicted by LowNodeUtilization is now placed in the same Node, evicted again by LowNodeUtilization, and placed in the same Node again.
Is there any option not to place them on the same Node?
assistant:
Hi @kahirokunn, the descheduler doesn't have any control over which nodes the newly-recreated pods will be placed on. It can only evict based on what it knows at the time, then it is up to the kube-scheduler to determine where new pods will go.

So when the descheduler evicts a pod from an overutilized node, it's assumed that the kube-scheduler will try to spread that pod onto an underutilized node (this is the kube-scheduler's default behavior). The scheduler could end up choosing the same node for a range of reasons such as affinity, taints, or even image locality in cases where nodes have similar utilization. You can try to adjust for this by tweaking the scheduler config and descheduler thresholds for utilization.

Can you share some more information about what you're seeing, such as your descheduler config, descheduler version, or a sample yaml from one of the evicted pods. We might be able to help out with that

---

@anil-venkata the descheduler and kubectl will report different values for memory. This is because the descheduler calculates usage based only on the requests/limits of pods, not what they are actually using (this is the same behavior the scheduler uses, so we remain consistent to ensure better performance during evictions). Kubectl reports actual memory usage from the metrics server.

A proposal to do this based on actual usage metrics is open here: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/issues/225

For more details, this has been discussed in several other issues, eg https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/issues/628 https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/issues/270 https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/issues/118
/close

Please see this pinned issue: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/issues/225 and the [LowNodeUtilization docs](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler#lownodeutilization):

> NOTE: Node resource consumption is determined by the requests and limits of pods, not actual usage. This approach is chosen in order to maintain consistency with the kube-scheduler, which follows the same design for scheduling pods onto nodes. This means that resource usage as reported by Kubelet (or commands like kubectl top) may differ from the calculated consumption, due to these components reporting actual usage metrics. Implementing metrics-based descheduling is currently TODO for the project.

Closing as duplicate, please feel free to continue discussion in the linked issue. Thanks!

@OuFinx glad that worked!

> Interesting, because this deployment(pod) doesn't have any PersistentVolume

Local storage is technically different from a PVC. From the docs section I linked:
> * Pods with local storage are never evicted (unless `evictLocalStoragePods: true` is set).
> * Pods with PVCs are evicted (unless `ignorePvcPods: true` is set).

Since you are using a local `mountPath` the descheduler doesn't evict those pods for any reason by default, since it's assumed the pods may need to mount the path on that specific node. Of course, this isn't always the case, so we added the option to evict them anyway.

If that's all set, I'm going to close this. Feel free to reopen if you have any more questions. Thanks!
/close

---

Just skimming your pod yaml, but off the bat the two differences I see that are maybe(?) causing this issue:
* The working pods have 1 container image, while the non-working pods have 2. I wonder if there is an issue in how we use container image names to identify unique pods (or are all of the non-working pods the same, with 2 containers?)
* The non-working cluster nodes have much higher resource usage, so maybe there is something preventing them from finding other suitable nodes (that isn't showing up in our current logging output).

It's also possible there's some other reason these pods are being ignored. If you change [`v: 4` in the helm chart](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/blob/f39058a/charts/descheduler/values.yaml#L58-L59) do you get any new logs about the pod evictions being prevented? (from https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler#pod-evictions)

---

Can you share the pod yaml two sample pods you are expecting to be matched as duplicates? RemoveDuplicates essentially tries to strictly identify duplicates by building keys of concatenated `Namespace/OwnerRef/Name/ContainerImages` (roughly explained in [this comment](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/blob/0ff8ecb/pkg/descheduler/strategies/duplicates.go#L118-L130)). So if all 4 of those don't match, the pod won't be considered a duplicated.

Hi @owenchenxy 
This is because the descheduler determines usage based only on pod requests/limits, not on actual real-time resource consumption. It does this in order to be consistent with the scheduler, which takes the same approach.

For this reason, the actual usage from commands like `kubectl top` (which uses kubelet metrics) may differ from the scheduler and descheduler's view. There has been some discussion on adding metrics-based real time usage descheduling, but this effort has not made much progress beyond initial proposals.

For more information, please see the pinned issue at the top of this repo: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/issues/225

(We also have a PR open to make this clearer in the documentation https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/pull/708)

I'm going to close this issue as a duplicate, please feel free to continue discussion on the topic in that main issue if you would like. Thanks!
/close

assistant:
Hi @mnorrsken,
Could you please share the logs of the descheduling runs you are seeing? (at level `--v=4` if possible). The descheduler doesn't just evict if nodes are underutilized, there must also be a node that is overutilized to evict from. In your screenshot, it seems like `mandalore` may be the one that is being seen as overutilized?

Also note that the descheduler calculates resource usage based on existing pods' requests and limits, so this calculation may differ from metrics-based "actual" usage reporting.

For rate limiting, that is more suitably controlled by adjusting the descheduling interval or cron run rate. You can also configure PDBs or set `maxNoOfPodsToEvictPerNode`, but if it is just one pod that is being evicted consistently then you may need to adjust your configuration to run the descheduler less frequently or modify your descheduler thresholds.

---

@mnorrsken glad that helps, in the future we do hope to add descheduling based on actual usage metrics. In the meantime I've opened a docs update to clarify this to help clear up future confusion. Thanks!

---

I think that I had too high expectations of descheduler "cleverness", its easy to think as a human that it's no idea to move around a pod that will cause over-utilization on every node it ends up on. Also the metrics of memory utilization that I see may not be the same as descheduler uses, causing confusing as it didnt seem to be over 50% at any point. I raised the limits and will keep verbose logging on, and also reduce the interval to see what happens.

## Permit descheduling of critical pods

We scale down our dev and test GKE clusters to 0 nodes for roughly 8 hours a day - over a month this is roughly a 50% cost saving.

The problem that we have is when we scale the clusters back up, kube-dns often gets multiple instances scheduled to the same node which can often prevent our other services being scheduled properly. This is because we use the smallest possible nodes, again, for cost saving reasons.

This is a known issue: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/52193 and was at one point solved with antiAffinity rules being added, however, the rules caused performance issues and were eventually removed.

Since the changes have been removed from kubernetes core and we can't modify the configuration of kube-system pods in GKE ourselves, the only other option seems to be to use descheduler, however, in the descheduler docs under "Pod Evictions" it says that critical pods can never be evicted: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler#pod-evictions

I can understand the reason why this is a sensible default but was wondering if the project would be open to a patch to allow this to be configurable?
assistant:
This is a reasonable use case, but like it was mentioned this could be dangerous to cluster stability if it's not carefully implemented and the effects are made clear to users: this will evict kube-system pods (really the only reason this is needed).

Given that risk and the fact that @roobert says there's no rush, I think it would be best to get this in for 1.20 (especially since we are trying to release 1.19 sometime next week). This way we can make sure there is thorough review and get input from more parties.

---

Or, you might put the new flag under [DeschedulerPolicy](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/blob/master/pkg/api/v1alpha1/types.go#L26). E.g.:
```
apiVersion: "descheduler/v1alpha1"
kind: "DeschedulerPolicy"
evictSystemCriticalPods: true << the new field here
strategies:
  "RemoveDuplicates":
     enabled: true
     params:
       removeDuplicates:
         excludeOwnerKinds:
         - "ReplicaSet"
```

The same could be done for the node selector and evict-local-storage-pods bits.

I was hoping it is utilizing the kubectl top nodes to calculating current utilization (which should reflect the results in the log with dynamically changing % of CPU & memory util at the moment).  Please clarify how is it calculating the current node resource utilization.

e.g. here is the data that I am talking:  in the log, I see this: Node “172.16.4.3" is appropriately utilized with usage: api.ResourceThresholds{“cpu”:52.5, “memory”:32.080248132547204, “pods”:41.25} but kubectl top node shows 172.16.4.3    2025m        25%       8699Mi          55% - meaning CPU 25%, memory 55% utilized

assistant:
@mossuchida descheduler follows what kube-scheduler does. As kube-scheduler works based on pods requests, descheduler also works based on pods requests. IMO, making descheduler work on real time utilization (like kubectl top) without kube-scheduler working on real time usage might lead to mismatch how pods are evicted by descheduler and then how replacement pods are scheduled by kube-scheduler.

But as soon as kube-scheduler starts supporting real time usage, descheduler would start that too.

---

> I don't understand why is the descheduler using the "requests". For me the idea of the descheduler was to balance the cluster when the requests where not properly assigned, for example, a service wasn't expected to consume 5Gi of RAM and a limit wasn't set for it. 

Descheduler only evicts pods but kube-scheduler does the actual scheduling of replacement pods. So if Descheduler acted based on anything other than requests, it would lead to mismatch between Descheduler and kube-scheduler as kube-scheduler would still schedule based on requests.   

>Balancing using the requests is already done by the Kubernetes scheduler, right?

kube-scheduler tries to balance only at the admission time for incoming pods, it does not rebalance already admitted pods.


> Without real-time the descheduler only tries to fill empty nodes, it doesn't try to save loaded nodes that are about to die due to resources.

No, descheduler does both as far as cluster has capacity. It tries to move pods from loaded nodes to less loaded nodes.

---

user:
## Add plugin for evicting BestEffort Pods from highly *actually* utilized nodes

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

When node is getting fully utilized, kubelet enters in a "self-defence mode" that starts to abruptly kill some kind pods. The first kind of Pods are BestEffort Pods (pods not setting requests), the second kind of Pods are memory under-provisioned  Burstable Pods. When these workloads are not meant to be best effort workloads,  this expected behaviour can cause reliability or performance issues in such workloads. 

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Descheduler could periodically check where these kind of pods are running. If they are running on Highly utilized (actual resources utilization) nodes, descheduler could evict such pods (so that they get the graceful termination) in a hope they will be scheduled in a less utilized node (of course this relies on kube-scheduler configuration)


**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Alternatives are:
 - User to set appropriate resource requests for all Pods that requires a minimum of availability/reliability
 - User make sure they workloads not setting requests are truly best effort workloads that can be killed at any moment without any eviction time


**What version of descheduler are you using?**

descheduler version: latest

**Additional context**

assistant:
There is some history about how `PodLifeTime` and `RemoveFailedPods` are to be interpreted. For that we created an issue to merge `TooManyRestart`, `RemoveFailedPods` and `PodLifeTime` into a single plugin: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/issues/1169. So (among other reasons) the interpretation can be simplified. Yet, due to insufficient capacity we have not been able to make it so.

Bringing this topic since it's not always clear what policy the descheduler should be expected to follow. One of the guiding principles is **ownership**. Given the descheduler does not create any pod it does not own any pod's lifecycle. Controllers creating pods (Jobs, RSs, RCs, SSs, ...) are responsible for making sure all such pods get properly cleaned. Also, [Garbage collection of Pods](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/pod-lifecycle/#pod-garbage-collection) mentions:
```
The Pod garbage collector (PodGC), which is a controller in the control plane, cleans up
terminated Pods (with a phase of Succeeded or Failed), when the number of Pods exceeds
the configured threshold (determined by terminated-pod-gc-threshold
in the kube-controller-manager). This avoids a resource l
[truncated]

---

The initial motivation for listing pods with `status.phase!=succceeded,status.phase!=failed` was to complement the kube-scheduler. By default the descheduler assumes any evicted pod is expected to be recreated and (re)scheduled. The [eviction policy](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler?tab=readme-ov-file#pod-evictions) can be adjusted to relax this constraint. Yet, the main focus has been primarily for rescheduling (`descheduler` and `kube-scheduler` working in pair even though both components are independent of each other).

Most of the strategies rely on setting the `status.phase!=succceeded,status.phase!=failed` field selector. `RemovePodsViolatingNodeTaints` ignores the field selector and lists all pods. Which got introduced by accident. Fixing through https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/pull/1688. So only `PodLifetime`, `RemoveFailedPods` and `RemovePodsHavingTooManyRestarts` are currently listing all pods.

Again, we need to consolidate all three strategies into a single one (through https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/issues/1169). To provide a reasonable API for the policies and to help with proper interpretation of what a descheduler is exp
[truncated]

---

The intention of the descheduler I have carried in my mind until now is to reallocate pods so they have a better chance of running elsewhere where they can run more efficiently, contributing to common cluster happiness, etc. Descheduling pods from nodes because of e.g. a security policies are not longer complied with is another such case as "Let's evict the pod from this node and have the kube-scheduler find a new node/home that still complies with the security policies". Even though there's no guarantee there's a node that complies with the policy. Leaving up to admins to make an informed decision that any workload deployed in a cluster has resources and complies with the policies. At least for a limited time.

I am still hesitant about including pods that completed their journey into eviction. It's paramount to emphasize the purpose of any of the descheduling plugins is not to clean up pods left around. So we don't invite undesired behavior. So as long as the requested functionality is used primarily (not necessarily exclusively) for evicting completed/succeeded pods so new pods can be rescheduled in their place, inclusion of the new state still aligns with the core purpose of th
[truncated]

See also: https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/naming/#labels

> CAUTION: Remember that every unique combination of key-value label pairs represents a new time series, which can dramatically increase the amount of data stored. Do not use labels to store dimensions with high cardinality (many different label values), such as user IDs, email addresses, or other unbounded sets of values.

The way Prometheus works, each of these individual pod names basically becomes a new metric, and they are all stored permanently in memory (in the descheduler's Prometheus handler). So this would be like a big memory leak, as it would be constantly growing and storing all of these single-point time series

assistant:
/close

@duronrulez currently the [LowNodeUtilization descheduler strategy](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler#lownodeutilization) uses [container requests](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/manage-resources-containers/) not the actual CPU and memory utilization. Yes, the word utilization is in the name of the strategy, and this is confusing. There is a one liner in the README that describes this, `Currently, pods request resource requirements are considered for computing node resource utilization.`.

See #7 for a discussion around utilization vs allocation terminology confusion. See #225 for a discussion on adding a new strategy that is based on actual container utilization metrics.

assistant:
This is a known limitation of the implementation. The descheduler will stop reconciling if there's less than 2 selected ready nodes in a cluster. This case got improved recently through https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/pull/1755. Starting 1.33 the descheduler loop will keep skipping until it sees at least two ready nodes again instead of returning an error. Thus, making the descheduler container run instead of terminate with an exit code.

---

Should `nodeSelector: "role=jjjjj,role=tttt"` be selecting 2 nodes but it is selecting only 0 (or 1) ?

assistant:
Hi @alifiroozi80 
Before going into details, I just want to point out that the `v1alpha2` api is not yet released. For descheduler, you should follow the docs on the release branch of your version (eg, v0.26.1 is on https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/tree/release-1.26). We are currently migrating from v1alpha1->v1alpha2 and the master branch is in-development.

To answer your question, yes filling in the policy in the values.yaml file is the best way to deploy it with Helm. The Policy config isn't a k8s resource (so you don't create it as a CRD). It is just a literal text file that you pass to the descheduler binary when it runs. In kubernetes, this can be done by creating a configmap with the config file as its data and using volume mounts to mount that configmap as a file in your Pod's container filesystem.

There are examples of doing this manually in the k8s manifests mentioned in the [quickstart guide](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/tree/release-1.26#quick-start). The Helm chart makes this a little easier by parsing the options from a single values.yaml file.

Hope that helps!
/remove-kind feature
/kind support

I strongly recommend updating to a more recent version of Kubernetes, as 1.20 is no longer supported by the community. To try to answer your question, it looks like the Node lister is actually getting some nodes once it [fetches them directly](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/blob/a6af54ab309d59adb43bedb86227cacdf1d71116/pkg/descheduler/node/node.go#L51) based on this log line, which I am assuming you truncated:

E0413 09:00:06.703838       1 server.go:46] v1.NodeList: Items: []v1.Node: v1.Node: ......
``` 

The message you're seeing is probably just indicating that the cache is empty (since this is a job and each iteration starts with an empty cache, that makes sense). If you're not seeing any other logs or pods evicted, it might be that there is just nothing to evict after that point.

---

LowNodeUtilization doesn't look at actual current resource usage (what you provided in your node details), it looks only at requested resources on the pods. These may differ from the actual usage on the node. Maybe that is why it's not evicting any pods.

I can't provide any support for the image you're using, `aveshagarwal/descheduler` is no longer maintained and has not been updated for several years. My advice is that you do not run this image, and instead update to a [recent release](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/releases) that is compatible with a more recent version of kubernetes.

Closing this as out of support. If you still see the issue on a more recent version, please open a new issue.
/close

If there is nothing to do, some strategies will log messages saying that (like this example in LowNodeUtilization: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/descheduler/blob/a6af54ab309d59adb43bedb86227cacdf1d71116/pkg/descheduler/strategies/lownodeutilization.go#L69). Other strategies might say nothing.

## Descheduler triggers infinite eviction/reschedule loop with Cluster Autoscaler and spot nodes **What version of descheduler are you using?** descheduler version: chart version is 0.31.0 **Does this issue reproduce with the latest release?** Yes **Which descheduler CLI options are you using?** `--policy-config-file=/policy-dir/policy.yaml --descheduling-interval=60m --v=3` **Please provide a copy of your descheduler policy config file** ``` policy.yaml: | apiVersion: "descheduler/v1alpha2" kind: "DeschedulerPolicy" profiles: - name: default pluginConfig: - args: evictLocalStoragePods: true ignorePvcPods: true name: DefaultEvictor - args: nodeAffinityType: - preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution name: RemovePodsViolatingNodeAffinity plugins: deschedule: enabled: - RemovePodsViolatingNodeAffinity ``` **What k8s version are you using (`kubectl version`)?**
kubectl version Output
$ kubectl version
Server Version: v1.31.8-gke.1045000
**What did you do?** We are using the RemovePodsViolatingNodeAffinity strategy to evict pods from on-demand nodes and schedule them on spot ones Scenario: 1. Some pods were initially scheduled on on-demand nodes because spot nodes were temporarily unavailable. 2. The descheduler evicted those pods due to node affinity preferences. 3. New pods were created and marked as pending. 4. Cluster Autoscaler saw that pending pods requi [truncated]
assistant:
If you use `preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution `, then I recommend enabling `nodeFit` check so that Descheduler only evicts them if there's another node available.

>As a result, pods end up again on the regular nodes, and the spot nodes that eventually come up remain unused.
Then Cluster Autoscaler detects the spot nodes are unused and scales them down.

Why is this the case? What is your Descheduler interval, and what is your Cluster Autoscaler scale down unneeded threshold? I would expect Descheduler to evict and put them on the Spot nodes, preventing the scale down.

---

This is unfortunate situation. The descheduler needs to evict so the CA can see Pending pods and scale up. In the problem description it's the kube-scheduler that schedules the Pending pods too soon. This could be solved by creating scheduling gates and waiting after CA scales up the nodes. Once the scheduling gates are lifted the pending pods targeted for the spot nodes can be scheduled. Yet, this requires another orchestration on the application side. In the best case the descheduler might suggest nodes need to scaled up. Yet, ultimately the kube-scheduler is the component responsible for making Pending pods assigned. Which could take longer (e.g. due to other scheduling constraints) than the waiting period before the nodes get scaled down again. So the descheduler is ultimately not sufficient in the suggested solution.

---
